does: This paper investigates jailbreak attacks against LLM-based robotic systems, exposing vulnerabilities that translate digital attacks into physical harm. The authors construct Harmful-RLbench, a risk-oriented benchmark for evaluating LLM-based robots using a human-object-environment framework, and conduct systematic measurements to understand attack feasibility. Their key finding is that traditional LLM jailbreak attacks fail directly in robot contexts because they must satisfy additional constraints: the adversarial prompt must be converted into executable policies that cause actual physical harm. The paper identifies two critical challenges unique to robotic jailbreaking—determining policy-executable optimization directions and accurately evaluating robot-specific safety failures—and proposes defense mechanisms tailored to these constraints.
connects: This work bridges two previously separate security domains: adversarial attacks on language models and robotics safety. It builds on extensive research into LLM jailbreaking techniques like prompt injection and adversarial suffixes, while grounding these attacks in the emerging challenge of embodied AI systems. The paper extends threat modeling frameworks from digital AI safety into physical domains, drawing on robotics literature around policy learning and safety constraints. It responds to growing concerns about deploying LLMs in autonomous systems where failures have real-world consequences, and connects to prior work on adversarial robustness in vision-based control and safe reinforcement learning, adapting those defensive approaches to the specific case of language-conditioned robot policies.
enables: This work establishes policy-executability as a fundamental constraint in LLM security for embodied systems, opening directions for more sophisticated adversarial attacks that account for physical realizability. The Harmful-RLbench benchmark enables standardized evaluation of defenses and serves as infrastructure for future research. The identified challenges point toward new research on constraint-aware adversarial optimization and physical-world verification of attacks. This work enables development of tailored defense mechanisms such as policy-filtering and execution validation layers specific to robotics. It motivates future investigation into the safety properties required for LLMs deployed in high-consequence domains, and suggests broader implications for other embodied AI systems like autonomous vehicles and industrial automation where translation from language to executable actions introduces novel attack surfaces.
